name: auth
context: MCP authorization via OAuth 2.1 — protected resource metadata, token exchange, PKCE, and bearer token usage for HTTP servers
info: MCP provides optional authorization at the transport level for HTTP-based servers. The mechanism is based on OAuth 2.1 with Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) for authorization server discovery, Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) for automatic client registration, and PKCE for authorization code protection. The flow: client requests without a token, server returns 401 with WWW-Authenticate header pointing to resource metadata, client discovers the authorization server, performs the OAuth flow, and includes the access token as a Bearer header on all subsequent requests. STDIO transport servers should use environment-based credentials instead. Token audience validation is mandatory to prevent confused deputy attacks.
triggers: add authentication to my MCP server
body:
# MCP Authorization

MCP provides optional authorization at the transport level, enabling clients to make authenticated requests to protected servers. Authorization is based on OAuth 2.1 and applies to HTTP-based transports only.

## When Authorization Applies

- **HTTP servers**: SHOULD use OAuth 2.1 per this specification
- **STDIO servers**: SHOULD NOT use OAuth; use environment variables for credentials
- **Custom transports**: MUST follow established security best practices for their protocol

## Roles

| Role | MCP Participant |
|------|----------------|
| Resource Server | MCP Server (accepts access tokens) |
| Client | MCP Client (obtains and presents tokens) |
| Authorization Server | Issues access tokens (may be separate from MCP server) |

## Authorization Flow

```
Client                    MCP Server              Auth Server
  │                           │                       │
  ├── Request (no token) ──→  │                       │
  │  ←── 401 + WWW-Auth ────  │                       │
  │                           │                       │
  ├── GET /.well-known/       │                       │
  │    oauth-protected-       │                       │
  │    resource ────────────→ │                       │
  │  ←── Resource metadata ─  │                       │
  │      (auth server URL)    │                       │
  │                           │                       │
  ├── GET /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server ──→ │
  │  ←── Auth server metadata ──────────────────────  │
  │                                                   │
  ├── POST /register (Dynamic Client Registration) ─→│
  │  ←── Client credentials ────────────────────────  │
  │                                                   │
  ├── Redirect user to authorize ───────────────────→ │
  │  ←── Authorization code ────────────────────────  │
  │                                                   │
  ├── POST /token (code + PKCE verifier) ───────────→ │
  │  ←── Access token + refresh token ──────────────  │
  │                           │                       │
  ├── Request + Bearer token → │                       │
  │  ←── MCP response ──────  │                       │
```

## Discovery: Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728)

MCP servers MUST implement Protected Resource Metadata. When a client sends an unauthenticated request:

1. Server returns `401 Unauthorized` with `WWW-Authenticate` header containing the resource metadata URL
2. Client fetches `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` from the MCP server
3. Response includes `authorization_servers` field with auth server URLs

```http
HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized
WWW-Authenticate: Bearer resource_metadata="https://mcp.example.com/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource"
```

## Discovery: Auth Server Metadata (RFC 8414)

Client fetches `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` from the authorization server to get:
- `authorization_endpoint` — where to redirect users
- `token_endpoint` — where to exchange codes for tokens
- `registration_endpoint` — where to register clients dynamically

## Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591)

MCP clients and auth servers SHOULD support dynamic registration. This enables automatic client registration without manual setup:

```json
POST /register
{
  "client_name": "My MCP Client",
  "redirect_uris": ["http://localhost:8080/callback"],
  "grant_types": ["authorization_code"],
  "token_endpoint_auth_method": "none"
}
```

## PKCE (Required)

MCP clients MUST implement PKCE to prevent authorization code interception:

```python
import hashlib, base64, secrets

# Generate verifier and challenge
code_verifier = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
code_challenge = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(
    hashlib.sha256(code_verifier.encode()).digest()
).rstrip(b"=").decode()

# Include in authorization request
auth_url = f"{auth_endpoint}?code_challenge={code_challenge}&code_challenge_method=S256"

# Include verifier in token request
token_response = requests.post(token_endpoint, data={
    "grant_type": "authorization_code",
    "code": auth_code,
    "code_verifier": code_verifier,
})
```

## Resource Indicators (RFC 8707)

MCP clients MUST include the `resource` parameter in authorization and token requests to bind tokens to the intended MCP server:

```
&resource=https%3A%2F%2Fmcp.example.com
```

## Access Token Usage

Tokens MUST be sent as Bearer tokens in the Authorization header on every request:

```http
GET /mcp HTTP/1.1
Host: mcp.example.com
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIs...
Mcp-Session-Id: abc123
MCP-Protocol-Version: 2025-06-18
```

- Tokens MUST NOT be sent in URL query strings
- Tokens MUST be included in every HTTP request (even within a session)
- Server MUST validate tokens were issued specifically for it (audience check)
- Server MUST NOT pass client tokens to upstream APIs

## Error Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 401 | Authorization required or token invalid/expired |
| 403 | Invalid scopes or insufficient permissions |
| 400 | Malformed authorization request |

## Security Requirements

1. **HTTPS everywhere** — All auth endpoints MUST use HTTPS
2. **PKCE required** — Prevents code interception attacks
3. **Audience validation** — Servers MUST verify tokens are intended for them
4. **No token passthrough** — Servers MUST NOT forward client tokens to upstream APIs
5. **Short-lived tokens** — Auth servers SHOULD issue short-lived access tokens
6. **Refresh token rotation** — For public clients, rotate refresh tokens on use
7. **Redirect URI validation** — Must match pre-registered values exactly
8. **Secure token storage** — Clients must store tokens securely

## STDIO Credential Handling

For stdio servers, credentials come from the environment:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "database": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["db_server.py"],
      "env": {
        "DB_PASSWORD": "secret",
        "API_KEY": "key-123"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

sections.title: OAuth 2.1 authorization flow
sections.body: The full flow: 1) Client makes unauthenticated request. 2) Server returns 401 with WWW-Authenticate header. 3) Client discovers resource metadata (RFC 9728) to find the auth server. 4) Client fetches auth server metadata (RFC 8414) for endpoints. 5) Client optionally registers dynamically (RFC 7591). 6) Client redirects user to authorize with PKCE challenge and resource parameter. 7) User authorizes, client receives authorization code. 8) Client exchanges code + PKCE verifier for access token. 9) Client includes Bearer token on all subsequent requests.

sections.title: Authorization server discovery
sections.body: MCP servers MUST implement Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`. This document includes `authorization_servers` listing one or more auth server URLs. The WWW-Authenticate header on 401 responses points clients to this metadata. Clients then fetch `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` from the auth server to discover endpoints (authorization, token, registration). This two-step discovery ensures clients always find the correct auth server.

sections.title: Token security and audience validation
sections.body: MCP servers MUST validate that access tokens were issued specifically for them — not for some other service. Clients MUST include the `resource` parameter (RFC 8707) in auth requests to bind tokens to the intended server. Servers MUST NOT accept tokens intended for other audiences. Servers MUST NOT forward client tokens to upstream APIs (prevents confused deputy attacks). Tokens must be stored securely, sent only via Authorization headers (never query strings), and should be short-lived with rotating refresh tokens.

sections.title: STDIO credential handling
sections.body: STDIO servers should NOT use OAuth. Credentials are passed via environment variables when the host launches the server subprocess. Configure API keys, database passwords, and other secrets in the server configuration's `env` block. This avoids the complexity of OAuth for local-only servers where the host process already has full control.

links.context: Auth applies to HTTP transport only
Servers implement resource metadata for auth
Clients perform the OAuth flow and present tokens
Auth sits in the transport layer of MCP

related.note: Authorization is part of the HTTP transport layer
Servers implement the resource server role
Clients implement the OAuth client role

author: dojo-community
